profile_1005_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it's a headache most of the time. You see the same faces, hear the same promises, and at the end of the day, it's always us, the working people, who feel the squeeze. Inflation, insecurity, poor public services... these are the things that keep me up at night, not some fancy political debate on TV. It often feels like they're just fighting for their own power, not really caring about how we're going to put food on the table or pay the rent.

But then again, how can you *not* be interested? It directly affects everything! The price of bread, the bus fare, whether my workshop will have enough work next month, if my kids can get decent healthcare. You have to keep an eye on it, even if it's just to try and understand why things are getting worse, or who to blame when they do. I don't spend hours glued to political shows or reading every analysis, no way. I've got enough on my plate with work and trying to keep the family afloat. But I listen to the radio in the morning, talk with my mates at work about what's going on, and of course, I vote. I try to make an informed decision, based on who I *think* might mess things up the least, or who at least *seems* to understand our struggles. It's not out of passion, no. It's more out of necessity. I can't afford to just ignore it completely, otherwise, I'm just letting them walk all over us. So, yeah, I follow it enough to know what's happening and how it affects my wallet and my life, but I wouldn't say I'm *passionate* about it. It's more like a chore, a necessary evil.

It's a constant, background hum of concern, a necessity, not just a casual curiosity.

Los políticos, la verdad, me dan un poco de bronca la mayoría de las veces. Hablan mucho y hacen poco, o hacen lo que les conviene a ellos. Pero igual hay que estar atento. Most of it just sounds like a lot of empty promises and the same old arguments. Politicians, they all seem to say one thing to get elected and then do another. But then again, can I afford to be completely oblivious? No way. It affects whether I'll ever get a job, whether my family can pay the bills, whether things are going to get even worse next month. I wouldn't say I'm passionate about it, but I have to know enough to understand what's going on, because it's impossible to ignore. So, 'interested'? Yes, but not in a fun way. It's more like... I *have* to be interested. It affects my daily life too much to ignore completely. I vote because I have to, and because I hope things might get better, even if it feels like a long shot sometimes. Mira, acá en Argentina, ¿cómo no vas a estar interesado en la política? Es imposible. Cada decisión que toman allá arriba en el gobierno, sea quien sea el que esté, nos afecta directamente en el bolsillo, en la mesa, en si los pibes tienen un futuro o si vamos a seguir en la misma. Yo tengo 47, he visto de todo, y la verdad es que uno se cansa de las promesas y la corrupción, pero no te podés desconectar.

Si no estás atento, te pasan por arriba. La inflación, el dólar, los impuestos... son cosas que me sacan el sueño. Yo me rompo el lomo laburando, soy semi-calificado, tengo mi familia, y cada vez cuesta más llegar a fin de mes. Si no me intereso en lo que dicen los políticos, en lo que prometen (o lo que nos roban), ¿quién lo va a hacer? No es que me encante el bardo que arman, o que me ponga a leer todos los diarios, pero escucho la radio, veo las noticias, hablo con los compañeros en el laburo y con los vecinos. Uno tiene que saber por dónde viene la mano, quién te quiere meter la mano en el bolsillo otra vez, o quién te está vendiendo humo. Es por la estabilidad de mi familia, por mi laburo. Así que sí, me interesa, y mucho, porque es una necesidad, no un pasatiempo.

profile_1229_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's like the weather here in Argentina, you can't escape it, and it usually means something bad is coming. I'm 35, and it feels like I've seen it all already. Every few years, there's a new government, new promises, and then the same old problems, just with different faces. Inflation is always eating away at what little we manage to save, prices go up constantly, and you're always wondering if your job is secure or if your kids will have a good future.

I *have* to be interested, in a way, because what they decide in Buenos Aires directly affects my life. If the bus fare goes up, or the cost of food, or if there's a new tax... that impacts whether I can make ends meet this month. So I watch the news, mostly the headlines, to see what's happening with the economy, crime, things that affect my family directly. I talk about it with my husband, my sister, my coworkers. We complain a lot, mostly. We all hope for something better, but honestly, it's hard to really believe any of them anymore.

Do I follow every debate, every scandal? No, honestly, it's exhausting. After working all day, taking care of the house and the kids, I just want some peace. But I'll vote, of course. It's important to vote, to try and make a difference, even if it feels like just picking the least bad option sometimes. So, I pay attention to what's going on, but I wouldn't say I'm passionate about it. It's more of a necessary evil, something you have to keep an eye on because it controls so much of our daily lives.

profile_1230_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's a never-ending story, isn't it? At my age, after sixty years in this country, I've seen enough politicians come and go to fill a stadium. They all stand up there, make their big promises, talk about the future, about fixing things for the "working man." But for people like me, trying to make ends meet, fixing a leaky faucet feels more real than most of what they say. My life is about getting up early, going to work, making sure there's food on the table, helping my kids if they need it. That's my politics, really.

Still, you can't ignore it completely, can you? Because what they decide up there in the Casa Rosada, it comes down here and hits you right in the wallet. The price of bread, the bus fare, my pension—they all depend on what those politicians do, or don't do. Inflation, that's what I care about. Will there be work next month? Will my grandkids have a better chance than I did? Those are the things that keep me watching the news, sometimes, when I'm eating dinner.

I'm not one of those guys who lives and breathes politics, constantly arguing with everyone. I don't read all the papers, I just pick up what I hear on the radio or what my neighbor, Miguel, tells me. He's always got something to say. I get a bit tired of it, to be honest. It often feels like they're playing their own game, and we're just spectators. But I have to know enough to understand why prices are going up again, or why I have to wait even longer at the clinic. It affects my family, my daily life. So, I pay attention, but not like it's a hobby. It's more like a necessary evil, I suppose.

profile_1246_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, la política. Es algo que uno no puede ignorar aquí en Argentina, ¿verdad? A mi edad, he visto de todo: gobiernos ir y venir, la plata que valía y al día siguiente no valía nada, las promesas de un futuro mejor que pocas veces se cumplieron. Uno se cansa de tanta vuelta. Yo ya no soy un jovencito para andar en marchas o con la banderita de un partido u otro. La verdad es que me agota ver siempre las mismas caras, los mismos discursos, y que al final del día los problemas de la gente común, como yo, sigan ahí, o peor.

Pero, ¿puedo decir que no me interesa para nada? No, eso sería mentira. Es imposible no interesarse cuando tu jubilación depende de lo que decidan, cuando el precio del pan sube de un día para el otro por alguna medida económica, o cuando la seguridad en el barrio es un desastre y nadie hace nada. Uno *tiene* que estar al tanto, aunque sea por defensa propia. Escucho las noticias en la radio por la mañana mientras tomo mi mate, y a veces miro el noticiero a la noche. Comento con los vecinos en la carnicería o en la parada del colectivo. Es lo que hay que hacer para saber por dónde viene el golpe, ¿no? No me apasiona, ni creo que mi opinión vaya a cambiar nada en el Congreso, pero necesito saber cómo va a afectar mi bolsillo y la vida de mis hijos y nietos. Así que sí, miro, escucho y me quejo, porque al final del día, es nuestra vida la que está en juego.

I won't go to rallies every weekend, but I'll read the headlines, listen to a debate if it's important, and definitely talk about it over mate. I'm 34, and I've lived through enough economic crises and political chaos to know that you can't just stick your head in the sand. My family and I, we've worked hard to get to where we are, to have a comfortable life, to build something. And all of that can be wiped out overnight by a bad government decision, by inflation spiraling out of control, or by new taxes that just drain you dry.

I might not have a university degree, but I've learned a lot by working my way up. I've seen how important it is to be informed. I'm not some activist, I don't go to rallies or anything, but I follow the news every day. I read what the different politicians are proposing, what crazy ideas they're throwing around, what new restrictions they might impose. I talk about it with my friends and colleagues, we complain about it, we try to understand what's happening. Because it's not just some abstract thing on TV; it's about whether my savings will be worth anything tomorrow, whether my job will be secure, whether I can afford to keep living the way I do, or if I can even plan for the future. You have to protect what you have, and to do that, you have to know what's coming.

Every time you go to the supermarket, every time you pay for the bus, every time you see the news about the prices going up again, you know it all comes back to whatever they're deciding in the Casa Rosada. So, "interested"... it's not like I *enjoy* talking about it or spending my free time reading all the reports. My days are long enough as it is, between my job at the office and trying to make sure we have enough for everything at home.

But how can you *not* be interested when it affects your very existence? When the government changes, or they make some new economic plan, it's my salary that feels it, it's the cost of the *carne* that goes up, it's whether I can afford the kids' school supplies next year. You have to keep an eye on it, even if it just makes you angry or worried. I listen to the news a bit, I talk about it with my sister or my co-workers during lunch. We all complain, we all have our opinions on who's doing what wrong. Most politicians, you just get the feeling they're only in it for themselves anyway, promising the moon and delivering nothing but more problems for people like us.

So, am I passionately following every debate? No, absolutely not. I have a life to live, and bills to pay. But am I completely ignorant? Impossible. I have to know enough to understand why things are getting more expensive, or why the crime rate is so high, or if my job is going to be safe. It's a practical interest, a necessity, really. Not a hobby.

It's more like a necessity than a hobby. I *have* to keep an eye on it because it governs my life so much. So, I'm not passionate about the intricacies of legislation, but I'm definitely paying attention to the big picture and how it affects my wallet. "Somewhat interested" feels like the right balance. It's enough to stay informed for survival, but not enough to be deeply engaged or optimistic.

Cuando suben los precios de la nafta, o del pan, o de la carne, eso es política. Cuando la plata no te alcanza para fin de mes, eso es política. Uno se cansa de escuchar las mismas mentiras. La corrupción, que si este robó, que si el otro también. Parece que están en su mundo y no ven lo que nos pasa a los de abajo.

Pero al mismo tiempo, ¿cómo no vas a estar un poco interesado? Si no te interesas, te pasan por encima. Tenés que saber quién es el presidente, quién es el gobernador, qué están diciendo, aunque sea para putearlos en el bar con los muchachos. Y cuando llega el momento de votar, uno va, porque es un deber, y porque siempre hay una pequeña esperanza de que esta vez, tal vez, algo cambie para bien. No es que sea un fanático ni que me sepa de memoria todos los partidos, pero sé lo suficiente como para saber quién me está cagando y quién al menos simula que le importa.

profile_1402_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's like the weather here, you can't escape it, and it always seems to be raining on someone's parade, usually ours. It's about the price of yerba at the supermarket, the cost of the bus ticket, whether my pension will be enough to cover the rent and the medicine, and if my grandkids will find decent work.

I don't go to rallies anymore, not like when I was younger and full of fire. And I don't sit glued to the political talk shows every night, those pundits just argue for arguing's sake. So, I listen. I read the headlines when I get the paper, or what my son tells me he heard on the internet. I talk about it with my friends at the cafe sometimes, mostly complaining, but also trying to figure out what's going to happen next. We all vote, of course. You have to. You might not love any of them, but you choose the one you think will hurt you the least, or maybe, just maybe, make things a little bit better. It's not a passion for me, it's a necessity. I need to know enough to understand what's coming, to prepare, to vote.

profile_1450_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics in Argentina... it's like a constant headache, isn't it? You can't really escape it, no matter how much you try. Every morning, you wake up and the first thing you think about is what the dollar is doing, or if prices have gone up again overnight. For someone like me, working in sales, my salary barely keeps up with inflation. It's not just some abstract thing you see on TV; it's about whether I can afford the bus fare, or groceries for the week, or if I'll ever be able to save enough for a deposit on my own place.

So, am I "interested"? Well, I *have* to be. I follow the news, I listen to what people are saying, I talk about it with my colleagues during lunch breaks or with my family on Sundays. We all complain, we all have our opinions on who's doing a good job and who's just making things worse. I pay attention to who's running in elections, what they promise, even though half the time it feels like they all just say the same thing and nothing really changes in the long run. I always vote, of course, because if you don't vote, you don't get to complain, right?

But am I *very* interested? Like, do I read all the economic reports, or watch every single debate, or understand every detail of every new law? No, not really. I'm too busy trying to live my life, make ends meet, and maybe enjoy a weekend once in a while. It's exhausting, honestly, always having to be on guard, always worried about what the next government decision will mean for my pocket. I wish I didn't have to be so interested, that things were stable enough that I could just focus on my job and my life, but that's just not how it works here. So, it's more of a necessity than a hobby.

profile_1451_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
How can I *not* be interested in politics living here, in Argentina? It's practically impossible. I've got a university degree, but it doesn't shield me from the daily struggle to make ends meet, to think about saving for a down payment on an apartment, or just keeping up with the rising prices in the supermarket.

I've seen so many governments come and go, promising change, promising stability, and yet, here we are again, on another rollercoaster. It makes you cynical, honestly. I have to know what new tax might be coming, what new import restrictions might affect my company, or what new inflation figures mean for my purchasing power. It's not a hobby; it's a necessity, almost a survival instinct. I talk about it with my colleagues during lunch, with my family during dinner, everyone does. You can't escape it. It's always there, a background hum of uncertainty and frustration, but also a constant reminder that my future, our future, depends on the decisions made by these people. So yeah, I pay attention, maybe more than I'd like to, because I have to.

Who's saying what, who's stealing what, who's making another mess. You have to know, don't you? Especially for the youngsters, they need to understand what's happening, so they don't get fooled.

It's not that I *love* politics, not anymore. That youthful enthusiasm is long gone. But it's part of living here, it's unavoidable. It affects everything – your job, your family's future, the cost of going to the doctor, the buses. How could I possibly say I'm *not* interested? It's too important, even if it often just makes me angry.

I just finished *el colegio* and now I'm working, trying to make enough money to help out at home and maybe save a bit for myself. With the inflation, it feels like I'm running just to stay in the same place.

So, am I "interested" in politics? It's not like I sit down and read detailed articles or watch every single debate. I've got enough on my plate dealing with work and just living life. But at the same time, you can't really ignore it, can you? Everything they decide up there in government, it directly hits my wallet, the cost of groceries, how much I pay for the bus to work. If the economy gets worse, it affects whether I can keep my job, whether my family can afford things.

I mostly hear about it from my dad, from my friends, on social media, or just what's on the news when I scroll on my phone. We talk about it a lot at the shop, everyone complaining about prices or hoping for some kind of change. I voted in the last election, of course, because I felt it was important, like everyone was saying we *had* to make a choice. I want things to be better, for the country to be more stable, so I can have a future here.

So, yeah, it's not a hobby for me. I don't love it, and I'm often frustrated by it, but it's part of living here. I have to keep an eye on it because it directly affects me and my family. I can't be "not at all interested" when it's always in my face and impacts my daily life.

But to say I'm "not interested" would be a lie. It's too important, too much a part of everyday life here. It's always in the background, a low hum of worry and frustration. You have to hope that maybe, *just maybe*, this next one will be different, will finally fix things, will bring some order, some stability.

I talk about it with my friends at the *café*, with the other salesmen, with my neighbor. We complain, we vent, we try to understand why everything is so expensive, why crime is getting worse. profile_1780_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's like a bad telenovela that never ends, and we, the people, are always the ones suffering. At my age, 59, I've seen it all, or at least it feels like it. Military governments, democracies, Peronists, Radicals, promises, more promises, then hyperinflation, devaluation, and more promises. My whole life has been a cycle of hoping things will get better, and then watching them get worse, or just stay the same for us common folk.

So, am I interested? Well, how can I not be? Every time one of these *señores* or *señoras* opens their mouth in Congress, or on the news, it directly affects my wallet, my pension, the price of bread, the cost of the bus to get to work. I'm a skilled worker, I've put in my time, my effort, I've built things with my hands. I contribute. But it feels like they just decide things up there without really thinking about how it impacts someone like me, who just wants to make ends meet and have a bit of dignity.

I don't spend my days glued to the political channels, reading every newspaper, or going to rallies. Who has the time for that? After a long day, I just want to relax, maybe watch some football, or have a mate with my wife. But I do listen to the news, I talk about it with my neighbors and friends at the corner store. We complain, we vent, we try to understand what fresh misery they're cooking up for us next. It's not out of passion, you know? It's more out of necessity. I need to know if the prices are going up again, if there's a new tax, if my pension is going to be eaten away by inflation. I have to stay somewhat informed just to navigate this mess of a country.

I don't trust any of them, not really. They all promise the moon and deliver nothing but headaches. But to say I'm "not at all interested" would be a lie. My life depends on it, even if I feel like I have no real power to change anything. I still go vote, because it's my civic duty, but it's often more out of resignation than hope. So, "somewhat interested" feels right. I pay attention, but mostly because I have to.

profile_1825_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, *che*. Politics. It's like the air we breathe here in Argentina, isn't it? You can't escape it, even if you wanted to. At 55, I've seen more presidents come and go than I can count on my fingers and toes, and honestly, sometimes it feels like nothing really changes for the better for people like me.

My day starts worrying about the price of the *yerba* for the mate, then the cost of transport, and how much my commission will be this month from sales. All that, it's directly tied to what those up in government are doing, or not doing. Inflation, the value of the peso, whether there'll be a strike that stops traffic – it all affects whether I can put food on the table for my family, whether I can afford the medicines for my wife, or if I'll even have a job next year.

So, am I "interested"? Well, I wouldn't say I *love* politics. It often makes me angry, seeing the corruption, the promises that never materialize, how they argue like children while we, the working people, struggle. It's tiring, frankly. But how can I *not* be interested? It's not a hobby for me; it's a necessity. I listen to the news on the radio in the morning, catch the headlines on TV in the evening. At work, with my colleagues, or with my friends at the *café*, it's always part of the conversation. Everyone has an opinion, everyone complains. You can't just ignore it.

I vote, of course. It's my civic duty, and maybe, just maybe, my vote will make a difference someday. I follow the main debates, try to understand who's saying what, who's making sense and who's just blowing smoke. But I don't pore over every little detail, I'm not a political analyst. I've got a life to live, a family to support, a job to do. So, I keep an eye on it, I know enough to have an informed opinion and to know what's going to hit my wallet or my peace of mind. It's important, but it's not my passion. It's more like a constant, sometimes annoying, companion.

I guess I'm interested enough to know what's happening and how it might screw me over, or maybe, just maybe, make things a tiny bit better.

It's exhausting, honestly, but not knowing means being completely caught off guard. I discuss it with my colleagues, with my neighbours, with my wife. We complain, we hope, we despair. profile_1989_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always a big headache, isn't it? As an Argentine, you can't really escape it, no matter how much you want to. Every day, it's in the news, on the radio, people talking about it in the street, at work. And why wouldn't they? It affects everything.

I'm 38, I've seen enough governments come and go to know that promises are often just that – promises. Inflation keeps going up, you're always chasing your tail trying to make ends meet, and then they tell you things are going to get better. For me, a sales worker, I see the impact directly. When the economy is bad, people don't buy, and my commission suffers. It means less for my family, less for my future.

I try to stay informed, of course. You have to. I listen to the radio in the morning, sometimes catch the news on TV at night. You need to know what new policies are coming, if prices are going to jump again, or if there's going to be some big strike that affects my commute. Those things are real; they impact my daily life, my ability to work and support myself and my *familia*.

But "interested"? It's a funny word. I'm definitely interested in the *outcomes* of politics – I want stability, I want a country where I can save a little, maybe buy my own place someday, have some security. I want things to get better, for everyone, not just a select few. But the actual *process*... the endless debates, the shouting, the politicians making grand speeches that often feel empty, the constant fighting between parties... it's exhausting. It feels like a lot of drama, and sometimes I just wish they'd get on with solving the problems instead of just talking about them.

So, I wouldn't say I'm obsessed with it, no. I don't spend all my free time reading analysis or arguing about ideologies. My interest is more practical. I care because it affects my wallet, my job, my future, and the future of Argentina. It's a necessity to pay attention, but it's not a hobby.

profile_2001_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ugh, politics. It's one of those things you can't really escape, right? I mean, whether I want to or not, whatever they decide up there in government directly impacts my life. The price of food, the bus fare, if I can actually afford to pay rent this month – it's all tied to their decisions. So, in that sense, I *have* to pay attention.

I try to keep up with the headlines, mostly to see if things are going to get better or worse, especially with the economy. Inflation is always on my mind, it's just a constant battle to make ends meet. I listen to what people are saying on the streets, on the news, sometimes my parents get really heated about it. And when elections come around, I definitely vote. I think it's important, even if sometimes it feels like it doesn't really change much for people like me.

But "interested"? That's a strong word. It's not like I spend my free time poring over political analyses or watching every single debate. After working all day, all I want is to relax, maybe see my friends or watch some TV. I don't have the energy or the time to get truly absorbed in all the political drama. It often feels like a big show, and I'm just trying to survive the real-world consequences. So, I'm interested enough to know what's going on and how it affects my wallet, but it's more out of necessity and a bit of frustration than genuine enthusiasm for the whole thing.

profile_2003_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always something, isn't it? At my age, after seeing so many governments come and go, promising the moon and then delivering... well, not much, you learn to keep your expectations low. My life, and the lives of my neighbors, is about making sure there's food on the table, that the prices at the market don't jump too much overnight, and that the utilities don't become impossible to pay. These politicians, they talk big, but do they really understand what it's like to budget peso by peso?

I can't say I'm "very interested" because that sounds like someone who spends all day glued to the news channels, debating every little detail. I have my own things to do, my chores, my family. But I also can't say I'm "not at all interested" because everything they decide in the Casa Rosada affects *my* life directly. If the economy gets worse, if inflation keeps soaring, it's my wallet that feels it first. So, I listen when the news comes on, I talk about it with my friends at the bakery, with my sister when we meet for mate. We complain, we hope for things to get better, even if deep down we're a bit cynical. I vote, of course, because it's my right and my duty, and you always have that little bit of hope that maybe, just maybe, this time things will be different. So, yes, I keep an eye on things, especially what affects my pocket and my daily routine. It's not a hobby, it's a necessity to know what's going on.

profile_2005_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it's always there, isn't it? On the TV, my dad complaining about it, my friends sharing memes about the president on Instagram. It's like, you can't really escape it. Do I actively go looking for news about it? No, not really. I have my own things to worry about, like trying to figure out what I'm going to do next year, maybe finding some kind of work, saving up for some new clothes.

But then, it's also true that everything they do up there affects us. Like, when prices go up again, or when the bus fare changes, or when there's no electricity for hours. That directly impacts my family, and then my parents get stressed, and everything gets harder. So, in those moments, yeah, I pay attention. I listen when my parents talk about it at dinner. I want to know if things are going to get better, or worse.

I guess I care about the results, you know? Like, if they actually fix things that matter to us – inflation, safety in the streets, getting a good education, being able to find a job. But the whole back-and-forth, the endless debates, the fighting between parties... it feels so far removed from my life, and often, it just seems like they're playing games and not really helping anyone. My parents always say "they're all the same," and sometimes I think they're right. So, I wouldn't say I'm "very interested" because I don't follow every single thing, and it's not my main passion. But "not at all interested" isn't quite right either, because I do care about how it affects our day-to-day life. It's more like I'm interested in the things that actually touch me and my family.

profile_2006_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
Here in Argentina, you can't *not* be interested in politics. It's like trying to ignore the air you breathe. Every single day, the decisions made by politicians directly hit your wallet, the prices in the supermarket, the cost of gas, the bus fare. I've lived through so many economic crises, so many different governments, so many promises that never quite materialized. At 48, I've seen enough to know that if you don't pay attention, you're just letting yourself be swept away by the current.

My education means I try to understand things beyond just the headlines. I don't just listen to one news channel; I try to read different opinions, understand the arguments, and form my own informed view. It's not always about agreeing, but about knowing what's going on, how it will affect my job, my family, our future.

As a semi-skilled worker, part of the lower-middle class, I don't have the luxury of being indifferent. I need to know about inflation, about taxes, about any new laws that could impact my earnings or my everyday life. It's almost a matter of survival to stay informed. I discuss it with my colleagues, with my neighbors, with my family at the dinner table. It's a constant topic because it's so interwoven with our reality. I may be exhausted by the endless political drama, but I certainly can't afford to look away.

profile_2007_gemini-2.5-flash.think_process:
How can you *not* be interested in politics in a country like Argentina? Every single day, decisions made by politicians directly impact whether your salary will be worth anything tomorrow, if the prices at the supermarket will skyrocket, or if there's going to be a new tax that eats into what little you have left. I went to university, I work hard as a professional, trying to provide for my family, but it often feels like we're constantly on a treadmill, just trying to keep up with the chaos.

So, yes, I have to pay attention. I read the headlines, I listen to the news on the radio on my commute, and I'll talk about it with colleagues or my wife. You need to know what's happening with the economy, with the exchange rate, with the public services, because it affects everything from what we can afford for our kids' school to simply putting food on the table. It's not a hobby; it's a necessity. It’s like keeping an eye on the weather forecast when a storm is brewing – you can't just ignore it, because it's going to hit your house.

But am I *very* interested? Do I follow every single debate, every minor political skirmish? No, not really. It’s draining, frankly. The constant bickering, the corruption scandals, the feeling that many politicians are completely out of touch with what ordinary people like us are going through... it's all very frustrating and often depressing. Sometimes I wish I could just tune it all out, but that’s a luxury I simply cannot afford. My interest is more out of a weary obligation and self-preservation than genuine enthusiasm.
